title: Repairing a loose chair joint with hide glue
subtitle: Re-glue a loose tenon or dowel joint in an antique or solid-wood chair using hide glue
excerpt: How to re-glue a loose mortise-and-tenon or dowel joint in a solid-wood chair frame, covering releasing the old joint, cleaning the glue surfaces, warming and applying hide glue, and cramping the joint until fully set.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (furniture). The Workshop Companion (1913). Practical Furniture Restoration (1920s).

glossaryTerms.definition: A protein-based glue made from animal hides, available as pearl granules or as a liquid. It is the traditional furniture-making adhesive and the only glue compatible with old hide-glue joints: the new glue bonds to the old residue that cannot be fully removed. It is reversible with steam or hot water, which allows joints to be re-opened without damaging the wood. It sets hard in 1 hour but reaches full strength after 24 hours.

A method for identifying loose joints: pick up the chair and shake it gently, listening and feeling for the click or movement of a loose rail. The rail that produces the rattle is the loose one. On a wooden chair, all joints should feel solid and the frame should not flex when pressed diagonally.

content.text: A chair joint that has worked loose can usually be re-glued without disassembling the whole frame. The key is using
: it bonds to the residual hide glue already in the joint without stripping the old glue out completely. PVA or epoxy will not bond reliably to old hide glue surfaces. The
locates the loose joint before any repair work starts.

Hide glue pearls: soak one tablespoon of pearls in cold water for 30 minutes, then warm the resulting gel in a small container over a pan of hot water until it is liquid (60 to 70°C). Do not boil: boiling breaks down the protein and weakens the glue. A small brush for applying the glue. Old cloth for padding the cramp jaws to protect the chair finish.
Step 1, locate and open the loose joint

Shake the chair and tap each rail to find the loose joint. Once located, open the joint by gently working the rail back and forth while pulling outward: the old dried glue will crack and the rail will ease out of the mortise or dowel hole. Do not force it: if the joint will not open, apply a few drops of warm water to the joint line and wait 10 minutes for the old hide glue to soften before trying again.

Scrape the old glue from the tenon and the inside of the mortise with the chisel. Use light strokes: the goal is to remove loose, powdery residue and leave a clean surface, not to cut fresh wood. Leave the thin layer of glue that is bonded directly to the wood fibres. Sand the tenon lightly with 120 grit to remove any glaze from the old glue. Check the fit: the tenon should slide into the mortise with light hand pressure and no wobble.
Step 3, apply the hide glue and reassemble

Work quickly: hide glue gels as it cools and must be applied at working temperature (60 to 70°C). Brush a coat of hot hide glue onto the tenon and into the mortise socket. Push the joint together immediately, twisting the rail slightly to spread the glue. The joint should close fully in one push.
Step 4, cramp and allow to set

Apply the cramp across the joint immediately. Pad the cramp jaws with cloth or cork to protect the chair finish. The cramp should apply firm, even pressure, not crushing force: hide glue is a surface-contact adhesive and does not need high pressure to bond. Check that the rail is aligned correctly before the glue gels. Remove any squeezed-out glue with a damp cloth before it dries. Allow the joint to set for one hour before removing the cramp, and 24 hours before putting weight on the chair.

If the tenon is loose because the wood has shrunk and the fit is sloppy, wrap the tenon with a layer of hide-glue-soaked fine linen before reassembling. The linen fills the gap and gives the glue more surface area to bond to. This is the traditional repair for a shrunken tenon and is reversible, unlike packing with wood strips.

A correctly re-glued hide glue joint is as strong as the original. Do not rock the chair for 24 hours after gluing. Store hide glue pearls in a sealed jar in a cool, dry place: they keep for years. Prepared liquid hide glue has a shorter shelf life of six to twelve months and must be kept refrigerated.

attrs.symptom: Joint loosens again within a few weeks of re-gluing
attrs.cause: The glue was applied cold and gelled before the joint was assembled; or the tenon fit was too sloppy for hide glue alone.
attrs.fix: Re-open the joint (warm water will soften the glue), clean the surfaces, wrap the tenon in linen soaked in hot hide glue, and re-assemble immediately. Apply the cramp before the glue can gel.

attrs.symptom: Joint will not open even with warm water applied
attrs.cause: PVA or epoxy was used in a previous repair: these adhesives are not reversible with water.
attrs.fix: Work the joint open mechanically using the mallet and a protected block. The timber may split before a PVA joint opens: work very slowly and in small stages.
